title: Chapter 7 Infographic: Perinatal Loss in Canada - Canada.ca
content:
## Chapter 7 Infographic: Perinatal Loss in Canada
Download in PDF format (120 KB, 1 page)
Organization: Public Health Agency of Canada
Date published: 2020-12-16
- Chapter 7: Loss and grief
- Chapter 7 Fact sheet: Family-Centred Care for Families who Experience Perinatal Loss
- Family-Centred Maternity and Newborn Care: National Guidelines
- Fact sheets and infographics: Maternity and newborn care
- 15.7% of couples have problems with fertility
- 15% to 25% of pregnancies end in a miscarriage
- Ectopic pregnancy occurs in approximately 1-2% of diagnosed pregnancies
- In 2014: Overall fetal mortality rate was 8.1 per 1,000 total births Fetal mortality rate for multiple births (13.5 per 1,000 total births) was much higher than among singleton births (4.8 per 1,000 total births) Congenital anomalies rate was for 430.5 per 10,000 live births Preterm birth rate (<37 weeks) was 8.1 per 100 live births Very preterm birth rate (<32 weeks) was 1.2 per 100 live births
- Overall fetal mortality rate was 8.1 per 1,000 total births
- Fetal mortality rate for multiple births (13.5 per 1,000 total births) was much higher than among singleton births (4.8 per 1,000 total births)
- Congenital anomalies rate was for 430.5 per 10,000 live births
- Preterm birth rate (<37 weeks) was 8.1 per 100 live births
- Very preterm birth rate (<32 weeks) was 1.2 per 100 live births
- In 2011: Infant mortality rate was 5.0 per 1,000 live births Neonatal mortality rate was 3.6 per 1,000 live births Post-neonatal mortality rate was 1.4 per 1,000 neonatal survivors
- Infant mortality rate was 5.0 per 1,000 live births
- Neonatal mortality rate was 3.6 per 1,000 live births
- Post-neonatal mortality rate was 1.4 per 1,000 neonatal survivors
- The leading causes of: Infant deaths: Immaturity (29.6%), Congenital anomalies (21.7%) Other/unknown (20.3%) Neonatal deaths: Immaturity (37.6%) Congenital anomalies (21.4%) Other/unknown (20.5%) Post-neonatal deaths: Congenital anomalies (22.3%) Other/unknown (19.7%) Sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) (19.6%)
- Infant deaths: Immaturity (29.6%), Congenital anomalies (21.7%) Other/unknown (20.3%)
- Congenital anomalies (21.7%)
- Other/unknown (20.3%)
- Neonatal deaths: Immaturity (37.6%) Congenital anomalies (21.4%) Other/unknown (20.5%)
- Congenital anomalies (21.4%)
- Other/unknown (20.5%)
- Post-neonatal deaths: Congenital anomalies (22.3%) Other/unknown (19.7%) Sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) (19.6%)
- Congenital anomalies (22.3%)
- Other/unknown (19.7%)
- Sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) (19.6%)
- Preterm birth rates among: Singleton was 6.3 per 100 live births Twin was 56.7 per 100 live births Triplet or higher order was 93.8 per 100 live births
- Singleton was 6.3 per 100 live births
- Twin was 56.7 per 100 live births
- Triplet or higher order was 93.8 per 100 live births
For references, consult Chapter 7: Loss and Grief in Public Health Agency of Canada. Family-Centred Maternity and Newborn Care: National Guidelines. Ottawa (ON): PHAC; 2020.
